FOREWORD
Its, indeed, a privilege to me 0 be invited to write a Foreword by way of
introducing to the public the third edition of the South Afican Tadian Who's
Perhaps this invitation by the Board of Management of the publication is
due 10 the fact tha 1 was long associated with the eitor and compile, the late
Mr. Dhanee Bramdaw, who brought out the first publication of its kind in South
Africa inthe year 1938.
1 well remember that when designing the first publication the late Me.
Bramdaw had frequent consultations with me as t0 the scope and the material
and general public information which were to include in it
I may be said that Mr, Bramdaw being a journalist of wide experience knew
his job of compiling and presenting & publication which would prove useful and
table to the public and also presentable from its get-up. ‘The first publication
ply proved all these combined efforts and the cover design and inclusion of
several photographs were beautifully done,
| know of my personal knowledge that the late Mr. Bramdaw used meticulous
care in planning & Venture and in executing and in completing it in praiseworthy
I know, also, he was complimented by both ‘Europeans and Indians on his
fit effort ‘The South ‘African indian Wh's Who as 2 reference book of Indian
People of South Africa has been used by the Government of India and also
‘thr oficial sources, a wel asthe public peneraly.
‘The present third eition brings the Who's Who uptodate. 1¢ contains
information about Indians who are prominent and whose combined conibotonstothe varios activites to promote the moral, soc, cational, cultray religous
ind politcal advancement of the community wil form a record of achievements
‘of wich any Community maybe proud
1 do hope and trast that this publication will yet matk another milestone in
the Geldof progress snd general advancement stated by the Indian Community
of South Altea. As South Africans we ae prowd of tis publication,
1 commend this third edition of the South Aftican Indian Who's Who to the
general public and I am sure that people will not only find it useful but interesting
find informative in all respects,
(S. R. NAIDOO)
Pictermaritzburg.
‘Tih Suly, 1958,a

PREFACE
‘THE third edition of the South African Indian
Who's Who is now presented to the public.
Since its inception and the publication of the first
‘edition it has fulfilled the purposes for which it was
intended. namely. to provide a documentary record
cof the achievements of Indians in the Union of South Africa and of those engaged
in trade, commerce and other professions.
‘The demand for such a publication thas not been confined to Indians only
but also includes Europeans who have commercial and other business contacts
wwith the Business Houses of the Indian Community.
Further, its circulation reaches far beyond the limits of the Union. A large
number of copies of this publication goes outside the Union of South Arica, thus
along a seady referent in ascertaining information about public, commercial
and professional personalities,
‘This issue will show what great strides the Indians within the Union have
‘made in all matters connected with religious, educational, social and sports matters
the life of the Indian community. Compared with other Communities elsewhere
is progress and evidence of self-expression and development and advancement
in a relatively short period, despite the many handicaps and hardships they have
hhad to endure, place them second to none in compatison with any other Com-
‘munity similarly placed elsewhere.
Me. DDhance Bramdaw who began this Issue of the Who's Who suddenly
met a tiagic end on the ath July 1982, while on a voyage to England. He was
then compiling this thd edition, but his end, regretted by'a large circle of fiends
and the publi, interrupted its publication for some time
‘This Issue is dedicated 10 hhis memory for his devoted and self-sacrificing
labours in the interests of his people.
I desire to place on record my sincere thanks to all. who assisted me in the
compilation of this Edition, Their unstinted support and help have enabled me
Jn a great measure to produce this publication.
Bramdaw Publications,
Natal, S.A.2s

DESAI, Pursbothamdas Giedhardas, 6. June 23, 1874, Porbander; died
Jan. 25,1952, at Durban. Relative of Mahatma Gandhi. Ede. Bombay University.
Came to Natal 1904, Contributed to “Indian Opinion”; was Eaitor of Gujerall
section, Courted Imprisonment during Mahatma Gandhi Passive Resistance Camn-
paign, Was Pret. of Bombay Presidency Asoc. Kathiawad Arya Mandal and
SA. Vanik Asoc; Patron Kathiawad Seva Samaj. Author of “Gitt” and
‘Mahatma Gandhi”. In recogstion of his work was asked by Indian Community
to uafurl fag of free India and address the gathering on First Independence Day.
GANDHL, Abbechand Amrita, b. Porbander, Nov. 8, 1870; died Nov. 12.
1955. Was nephew of Mahatma Gandhi. Came to Natal 1856. Benefactions
include substantial donations 10 Vishwaroop Temple, Toagaat High School and
‘Sasti College. Profoundly religions. Founder of firm Gandhi & Co. (Pry.) Lid

> BABU HAZURA SINGH €
INGH, Babu Hazurs; b. Rannia, Dist. Ludhiana, Punjab, 1879; educ. Ludhiana Mission
High School, matriculating from Punjab University, 1897, started study of medicine at the
Medical College at Agra, but owing to ill health left college, Came out to East Afsca in 1898
‘and entered railway service, In 1900 arrived in Durban. After two years’ stay returned to
India. Came back to Durban 1904, then proceeded to the Transvaal, where he took up per-
‘manent residence. Was for a period a sub-railway contractor, later’ opened general dealer's
business. From 1919 to 1925 conducted wholesale business in Johannesburg, Public activities:
always took an active part in Indian public affairs; identified himself with the passive
resistance movement under Mr. Gandhi, ‘suffered several terms of imprisonment, and continued
to take a share right 10 the end, concluding with the 1914 settlement, In 1914 went back to
Punjab, here rendered war service by recruiting soldiers for France and Mesopotamia. Started
primary school in Germiston which has since received Government recognition. A liberal patron
{0 all public efforts for community welfare.

HAFFEJEE'S
FURNITURE MANUFACTURERS
‘The first of the Haffejes to arrive in this country was Hoosen KhatiD who
embarked from the island of Mauritius, on board the S.S. Attia, in 1888. He
Settled in Durban for a while where he had a trading store in Gardiner Street,
opposite the General Post Office. He later moved to lxopo and over the years, |
ished several branches in country areas, at Bizana and Howick, Natal. He |
in the 30's, He died in 1931]
Mahmood Hoosen Haffejee, the fourth son of Hoosen Khatib, was born at
Ixopo in 1899, He moved to Durban and established a light furniture factory in
1914 in the central area. During the depression he had several hundred workers
in his two adjoining factories in Lorne Street and specialized in shopfitting and
mass-produced furniture. He was a pioneer amongst the early Muslims in the
furniture field. He later moved to Kilkenny Road, Mayville and in 1954, to Bellair
Road, Cato Manor. Expropriation, however. had a considerable effect on further
expansion and the factory continues under Council tenancy to this day, until new
premises are built in the near future in the Isipingo industrial complex.
In 1948, Ahmed Haffejee, the eldest son of Mahmood Hoosen Haffejee,
joined his father in business and by 1958 had added a new division to the factory.
‘The manufacturing of Kitchen furniture was undertaken and the expansion has
continued to such an extent that the Hafiejee Furniture Manufacturers is now
acknowledged amongst the top 5 in the Republic in this field. The factory con-
tinues to manufacture its other products.
Alter the death of his father in 1968 Ahmed Haffejee assumed complete
control of the manufacturing concern. He was joined by his brother, Hafez
Mohamed Hoosen, who has now become the Production Manager and is also a
partner in the firm, The firm produces close on a thousand Kitchen units each
month and the factory turn-out of other furniture products puts it ahead of all
its rivals, Wooden suitcases and trunks manufactured by the firm are directed to
the lucrative Pondo trade as well as to local needs.
The little factory started by Mahmood Hoosen Hatfejee in 1914 is today
making its contribution to the industrial development of this province and with
the expansion envisaged, for the not too distant future, will continue to play its
part in an ever expanding Natal economy.

ABDULLA ISMAIL KAJEE
Abdulla Kajce was undoubtedly one of the outstanding Indian personalities of the‘and forty
‘Asa politician and a businessman he was remarkable. He was ane of the few men to
have made sucess of both, snd that, under extremely trying conditions

lay apparently lightly on his shoulders and if it became synonyrnaus with the name
Abdulla Kajee, one, could be pardoned for thinking that way. He was Congress Il
He was of the opinion thatthe Indian had to fear tive anicably with the country’s
Whites und to be able 10 do s0 one would have o establish good relations with them,
‘Towards this end he devoted his life. What “A135 he was stectonately called, feared
most, was a breach in Tado-White relations
Not only was Abdulla Kaje eloquent, he was also forceful in everything he did, He
was gifted wih a fine political acumen and an intelligence above average. His achievements
in business and politics speak for the man. He was no respecte of persons His speeches
were as fery as his temper. He was a true democrat, He would tlk to anyone at any
fime. The poverty-stricken and the wealthy. to his mind, were equally deserving of his
undivided attention in his dally contact with people

‘ABOOBAKER H.A. JHAVERY
Ihavery Aboobaker Hajee Ahmed, Merchant, b. 1850, Porbandar, India. Educated at
Porbandar, Saurastra, India. Arrived ia South Afica in 1872, the frst of the Indian
Merchant cass to set foot in this country, He was the fore-unner to the thousands of
Muslim traders who finally settled in South Africa. A former Merchant of Maunitus, he @@
‘ame to this country in response to requests from the Natal government for supplies of
‘Spices and Indian trade goods for the requiements of tndian immigrants in Natal. He
rived here with a huge consignment of these goods in 1872. He later extended his
Activities to the Transval. He was one of the easly property owners and in the absence
of ant-indian legislation held tie deeds in prohibited areas here, and inthe Transvaal.
Sho had business interests in Maurits and India. He ded during the Cholera epidemic in
Bombay in 1887.
BLK. KHAN
Barrsterat-Law, Member of Lincala’s lon, the Supreme Court of Judicature and an
‘Advocate ofthe Supreme Coett of South Africa, Rai Karim Khan was born in 1874, in
Bombay, India. Its not known when he settled in this country but to all accounts, he
Wwasone ofthe outstanding men of the twenty's In the politcal arena, he seed for many
Yearsas Joint Secretary of the NLC. ane eepresented Natal at various South African Indian
Conferences. During the Anglo-Boer Wat he joined the forces aa non-<ombatant in the
“Ambulance Corps. and saw service at Colenso and at the battle of Spionkop. He was
recipient of the Queen's Medal for meritorious service. He was appointed by the Govern-
nent of Natal to the Board of Examiners for Indian Languages and served in this capacity
for many years.
“Today, the name R. K. Khan is associated with one of the most modern hospitals built

fellow-man. To what purpose could he best make use of his ast estate, must have been

was lef to provide for Indian ‘Over the years the R. K.Khan Hospital and

J T -is with sincere pleasure ‘that I wvité a foreword for the South A frican Indian
Who's Who-and Commercial Directory which has been compiled by Mr. Dhanee Bramdaw of
Pietermaritzburg. Myo Bramdaw is not only a-valued personal friend, but isin my opinion
one of thé ablest of the younger genération of Indians in South Africa. Genius has been
defined by a well-known writer as an-infinite capacity for taking pains. If this tsa true
definition, Mr. Bramdaw has Senius,; for to my knowledge he has spent much time and taken
greal pains over the production of this directory.
Mr. Bramdate's compilation, whose artistié value is enhanced by a large nimber of
photographs, should be of great value not only to persons engaged in business but to the South
Afritaw world al large; both European and Indian, and also to the Union Government, since
it shows in a-compact form the important part which the Indian-has played in the progress
and development of this sub-continent,
A glance at the book will-also make clear, what is often not sufficiently ap preciated by
Europeans, that the cast majority of Indians in ihis’ country have been born in South Africa
and know no-other home..-The Government of India and the increasing number of people
in India who take an intérest in-the welfare of their fellow-coitntrymen overseas will also
find much io interest them in-these pages.
I conimend this inost itseful compilation to the public of South Africa and India in the
hope that time will bring these two great countries into closer and more friendly relations
with each other.

INGH, Babu Hazura ; 6. Rannia, dist. Ludhiana, Punjab, 1878 , edue. Ludhiana Mission High
School, matriculating from Punjab University, 1897, started study of medicine at the Medical
College at Agra, but owing to ill health left college. Came out to East Africa in 1898 and entered
railway service. In 1900 arrived in Durban, After two years’ stay returned to India, Came back to
Durban 1904, then proceeded to the Transvaal, where he took up permanent residence. Was for a
period a sub-railway contractor, later opened general dealer's business. From 1919 to 1925 conducted
wholesale business in Johannesburg, Public activities. Has always taken an active part in Indian
public affairs , identified himself with the passive resistance movement under Mr. Gandhi, suffered
several terms of imprisonment, and continued to take a share right to the end, concluding with the 1914
settlement. In 1914 went back to the Punjab, here rendered war service by recruiting soldiers for
France and Mesopotamia. Started primary school in Germiston which has since received Government
recognition. A liberal patron to all public efforts for community welfare. Address » P.O, Box 292,
Stand 872, Asiatic Bazaar, Phone 51-2251, Germiston, Transvaal.

PREFACE
.
7 HE SOUTH AFRICAN INDIAN WHO'S WHO AND COMMERCIALDIRECTORY is fulfilling the purpose for which it was intended, namely,
to provide a work of reference regarding Indians engaged in trade,-commerce
and in the professions.” The demand for sucha publication has not been
confinéd to Indians but also includes Europeans wha have commercial and
other contacts acith Indians. dyain its circulation is not only Union wide
but a large number of copies go-overseas, so that the world might know of
South’ Afriea’s Tadian commercial and “professional personalities and
public men,
The fact that the size -of the book, which ts only in its-second issue,
has doubled itself is another proof that it-has pustified its existence, as yell
as tis ¢laim of meeting the need for-an Indian reference publication for
South Africa and the adjacent territories:
We have wandered farther afield since the first issue and the result
of aur labours forms -a valuable section of reference concerning Indians of
Southern and Northern Rhodesia, Nyasaland and Portuguese Kast “Africa.
Even for Indians themselves THE WHOS WHO has viteliséd names that
must be useful for the purposes of information in connection with trade as
well as the projected movement of Closer co-dperatian of the three British
countries. “There is a community of interest among the people in these areas
as well ax of those of Portuguese East Africa, that we felt they should have a
section to themselves.
The preparation of this poblication; hich we-first envisaged would
bea matter of its reappearance “once every tio yearsy has taken® far too
much time and vite cannot, for the present, promise future publications
more frequently than once every three years. “Inose planning it awe hare in
mind that each volume should become more and nore useful and indispensable,
particularly to con mercial interésts.
We desire to place on record our appreciation and thanks to all thase
who have assisted in the compilation of records; not only in the Union but
in the Rhodesias, Nyasaland and Portuguese East Africa: [tis due to their
unstinted support that this book has achieved so-great a success.
The Natal Witness. DHANEEF BRAMDAW
Pietermaritzburg.
October, 1939.

Established July 31, 1927 P.O. Box 4, LIMBE, Nyasaland

THE JHAVERI FAMILY
i BOOBAKER HAJEE AHMED JHAVERI; . Porbandar, 1850: died
| in Bombay. Aug.. 1887. Came to South Africa from Mauritius in 1873, being
first Indian, following in the wake of the Indian immigrants, to start business
Durban in 1872. He also extended his activities to include the Transvaal, where
he acquired landed properties. which until recently, were the only land registered
in an Indian's name in that Province. During his short stay in this country, he
became a popular member of the Indian community. He was of a public-spirited
nature, generously contributing towards the upkeep of several institutions. | @n-
fortunately he became a victim of a cholera epidemic then raging in Bombay, where
he passed away at the early age of 37. He was sucvived by his only son, Hajec
Ismail Aboobaker Jhaveri
HAJEE ISMAIL ABOOKAKER JHAVERI ; . Durban, 1878: died Durban, 1925. When three years of age
went to India, remaining till 1913. On his return to South Africa he was engaged in business ; also connected with
the firm of the East Indian Trading Co. Re-visited Porbandar in 1916, where he took a prominent part in public
activities, becoming the leader of the Muslim community ; presiding at the Kathiwar Aluslim Fducational Society at
Porbandar in 1918. Was the chairman of the Porbandar Jooma Mosue Managing Committee and of the Madressa
Managing Committee. Was highly respected by all sections of the community, among whom included His Highness
the Rana Sahib of Porbandar. He was an esteemed friend of Mahatma Gandhi. He died at the age of 47, leaving a
widow and two sons. (Centre)
ABOOBAKER HAJEE ISMAIL JHAVERI, recipient King George V Silver Jubilee Medal, 1935, and King George
V1 Coronation Medal, 1937 ; 6. Porbandar, Sept. 21, 1900 ; ¢. s. of late Hajee Ismail Aboobaker Jhaveri ; m Rabia,
0. d. of Khan Bahadur Hajee Cassim Adam, of Pretoria; educ. Porbandar and Marine College, Durban, Public
activities : Chairman of the Reception Committee of the All-India Memon Conference (4th Session at Porbandar),
1935, also patron of the Conference ; life member, Memon Educational Society, Calcutta ; life member of the Rajkot
‘Memon Boarding House .. patron of the Young Men's Memon Assocn., Rajkot; chairman and life member, Durban
Memon Assocn., 1938; led Memon deputation before Sir Abdullah Haroon in Karachi in connection with public
affairs ; life member, Johannesburg Memon Society. Recreation » Sports and travel. Clb » Orient (Durban) and
Machonochie (Porbandar). Address > (In India) Jhaveri Buildings, Porbandar. (In Durban) 160, Grey Street,
Durban, Natal
MAHOMED HAJEE ISMAIL JHAVERI ; 5. Porbandar, March 16, 1918; v. s. of late Hajee Ismail Aboobaker

hamed ; 6. Olpad:

Olpad and Bulsar
Came to South Africa
carly in 1900, Settled

Has taken active part

ucational, municipal
and Parliamentary. As
soon as he arrived. he
recognised need. for
some elementary educa

Mr. Gandhi's attention to the fact in 1907. Sub-
sequently he helped in the establishment of what is
now the S.A.L.C, Sponsored other conferences in
the Cape to oppose anti-Indian measures in 1916 and
other dates. ‘Took prominent part in consolidating
the position of Indian traders as to their licences
‘Asa public man he has come into contact with members
fof the Government and Legislature. He saw need
of an Indian Agency in South Africa and submitted
a statement to this effect to the Paddison Deputation
in 1925. “Has appeared before numerous commissions
and was thanked by the Paddison and other
deputations for his information on Indian questions.
Had consistently opposed the condonation and other
schemes, which he considered would reduce the
Indian population. Is secty. of the Bababia Muslim Soc.

chairman, Cape Indian National Assocn. ; was appointed
leader to South African Indian Federation ; member,
Coloured and European Joint-Council :' member,
South African Party (Peninsula) Council ; executive
member, Indian Traders’ Assoca. ; chairman, Cape,
Wynberg and District Sports Club; member, Literary
and Debating Society and ‘of other institutions.
‘Address : 12, Chester Rd., Walmer Estate, Cape Town.

Public activities : Ever since arrival has been engaged
in public activities and was a co-worker with Mr.
Gandhi for several years and an original member of
the Natal Indian Congress. Member of the deputation
which interviewed the Cabinet in 1924 in connection
with the Boroughs Ordinance. A member of the South
African deputation to India in connection with the
agitation against the Class Areas Bill, 1925. A keen
and intelligent public worker and an enthusiast in
bringing together the various races for the purpose of
securing mutual understanding and goodwill. Was
hon. treas. of the Indian Committee organised by the
then Mayor, the late Mr. Nicholson, in the Council
Chamber in 1915 for Indian community's contribution
to the War Funds. In 1936 went on pilgrimage to
Mecca, Medina, Jerusalem, Damascus and other
holy places, and then visited Egypt, Turkey, Greece,

SANNYASI, Bha-wani Dayal; Com-missioner for Oaths ;
6. Johannesburg. Sept.
10; 1892; s, of late
Jairam ‘Singh, ex-
presdt. Transvaal In-
dian Assoen. ; educ. at
Bihar. Leaving South
Africa 1904, returned
1912. On return elected
presdt. Young Men's
Indian Assocn., Ger-
miston, 1913; ‘one of
the organisers of the
Indian Strike at New-
castle in 1913 and was
sentenced to term of
three months” im-
prisonment with hard labour, with his wife Jag-rani
Devi and child Ramdutt. Edited Hindi section of
The Indian Opinion in 1914, Dharma Vir 1917-18,
Aryavarta 1931-32. Organised the first and second
South African Hindi Literary Conferences in Lady-
smith and Pietermaritzburg in 1916 and 1917 respec-
tively. Elected a member of Bijolia-Mewar (Rajputana)
Commission to investigate grievances of the passive
resisters in 1920. Attended and participated in many
other political, social, literary and religious conferences
during his short stay in India, Founded Jag-rani
Press at Jacobs in 1922, Edited, printed and published
the weekly Hindi in Hindi and English from 1922 to
1925. Repstd. South African Indians at Indian
National Congress, Amritsar 1919, and Gaya 1922
and was instrumental in getting The S.A.1.C. affli-
ated to The Indian National Congress. Elected a
member of the South African Indian Deputation to
India in 1925. Established the Pravasi-Bhawan
consisting of a fine library and free school at Bahuara,
Khargarh near Sasaram (Bihar) in 1926. Admitted
into the Order of Sannyasis in 1927. Acted as a
representative of the S.A. Indian Congress to study the
working of the New Immigration Act in the districts
of Utrecht and Vryheid rendering valuable assistance.
Elected presdt. of the First Indian Overseas Conference
held at Brindaban (Muttra) in 1930. Elected presdt.
of Shahabad District Congress Committee and was
sentenced to simple imprisonment for two and a half
years under section 124 (a) of Indian Penal Code for
delivering seditious speecnes. Placed in Class A and
served his term in Hazaribagh Gaol. Released in
1931 unconditionally by amnesty after serving one
year. During the period edited a hand-written monthly
magazine Karagar in the Gaol. Published “a report
on the emigrants repatriated to India under the
assisted emigration scheme from South Africa and on
the protlem of returned emigrants from all colonies’”
jointly with Pandit Benarsidas Chaturvedi in 1931
and subsequently published the “public opinion on
the assisted emigration scheme under Indo- South
African Agreement.” Presided at the All-india Hindi
Journalists’ Conference at the Senate Hall, Calcutta
University in 1931, Presided at tenth Bihar Provincial
Hindi Literary "Conference at Baidyanathdham
(Deoghar) in 1931. Author of many Hindi books,
namely : History of Passive Resistance in South
Africa, My Experiences of South Africa, Story of
My Prison-life, Biography of Mahatma ‘Gandhi,
Indians in Transvaal, Natalian Hindu, Vedic ReligionINDIAN WHO'S WHO
and Aryan Culture, Educator and Cultivator, The
Vedic Prayer, etc. Elected Honorary Indian Probation
Officer ; v.-presdt., N.LC. since 1921. Founder of
many political, sociat, religious and literary societies
both in South’ Africa’and India. Single-handed con-
ducted, in India, strenuous agitation against proposed
segregation measure by Union Govt, 1939: was able
to carry the whole country with him, from H.E. the
Viceroy to the peasant. Address : P.O. Jacebs. Tel.
add. : "‘Sannyasi,” Jacobs, Natal, South Africa

On leaving college came to Southern Rhodesia, joined

came to Bulawayo, started retail business in partnership

dissolved partnership and continued as sole proprietor

chant, Branches at Luanshya, Northern Rhodesia |

Has buying offices in Londoa, Bombay and Kote
Public activities; Ever’ since arrival in Southern
Rhodesia has prominently associated himself with
the public activities of his community : occupying the
highest positions—Chairman of the Pulawayo British
Indian Assn. since I chairman of the School
dvisory committee of the only Government Indian
school in the Colony since 1933. Chairman of the Bula-
wayo District Cricket Union. Represented Rhodesia
at the S.A.LC. Conference at Kimberley, 1928: only
Indian recipient in Bulawayo of the King George
Jubilee Medal and the King George VI. Coronation
Medal. One of the founders of the Arva Mithra
Mundat, and its chairman for a period ; was elected
chairman of the first Rhodesian Hindu Conference,
held at Salisbury in 1936. Presided over Indian
gatherings held in honour of visiting eminent Indians,
including Mrs, Sarojini ‘ Srinivaca
Sastri and Kunwar Sir Maharaj Singh. Address

L. K. HURWITZ & SON (PTY.) LTD.SOUTH AFRICAN CYCLE SUPPLY Established 1903S.A. INDIAN COMMERCIAL DIRECTORY 73a.
a prominent part in the Society were the late Mr. Kalanji Gopalji, who supervised and collected the rent
of the cottages belonging to the Society until the time of his death, and also the late Mr. /A. L. Billomoria,
who contributed very liberally to all the funds and held a responsible position in the Society.
The aim of the Society is the upliftment of the meiibers in cultural and religious fields, to further the
national cause of the Motherland and National Institutions... The Society was also represented on the
Transvaal British Indian Association.
The following are some of the many activities of the Society —
The Society, represented by the late Mr. Parshottani Vallabh Bhagat (Caaivman) and Mr. Prag Prema
{Seéretary), was a signatory to the illuminated Address presented to Mr, Polak by the Transvaal British
Indian Association.
The Society also assisted The British Indian Association financially and morally.
‘A substatitial dofation toe the Patidar Boarding House at Surat (India) on an uhdertaking being given
by Mr, Vithalbhai Mehta, that the students of every community would be permitted to reside in the Boarding
House.
A donation of approximately £200 to assist the erection of a Dharmashala at Unai, a sacred place in
India. . This provides accommodation for the pilgrims of the community.
A present of £20 which was given to Mr. Narctidas Vithaldas Damania, in appreciation for the services
he rendered to the Hindoo Community by erecting a Hindoo Crematorium in Johannesburg.
The Hindoo Crematorium was equipped with the necessary furniture by the Society:
A dénation of £37 10s. Od. to the Orrisa Famine Fund. -(India.)
A donation of £37 10s. Od. for the rélief of the victims of thé Jaliariwala Bag (India) (through Mahatmaji)
A donation of £135 to the Tilak Swaraj Fund {through Mahatmaji)-
A dotation of a purse of £50 to Pandit Ishverdatta Vidyalankara, -(Gurukur Kangari.)
The purchase Of a Stand for the sum of £250 in the 12th Street, Vrededorp, Johannesburg.
it was mainly through the efforts of the Society that Mr Dalpatram Vanmalibhai Darji arrived from
India as an educated immigrant.
A donation of a purse of £26 5s. Od. to Mr. Shastriji for the Servant of India Society.
Inthe year 1927, the Society decided to erect a Hall on thé Vrededorp Stand, and although the members
contributed to this fund very liberally, it was later argued by some of the members that cottages should be
erected on the Stand. Thus a dispute arose in the Community, and the Society ceased functioning until
1933, However, thanks to the efforts of Mr. Govindbhai Chbibabhai, the Society then again started to
function, and since then its activities were as follows :-—~
A’Drait of £49 3s. 0d. was sent to Babu Rajendraprasad, Patna (India) for the relief of the victims— DEPARTMENTS —
CYCLES, CYCLE ACCESSORIES RALEIGH CYCLES
MOTOR “ACCESSORIES JOHN BULL -TYRES
GRAMOPHONES AND SEWING Agencies —
MACHINES .— PRAMS, BeS. AL CYCLES
TOYS AND FOLDING .CARS HERCULES. CYCLESSole Agents for— of the earthquake in Bibar.
In the year 1936 the Society decided to erect cottages on the Stand, and members contributed approxi-
mately £900 towards this fund. At present the Society owns four cottages, and has utilised one of the
cottages for the purpose of a Hall. Numerous meetings and other functions have been held in this Hall, which
has been a tremendous asset to the Community. The cottages are let and are thus a source of income to the
Pand.
‘The Society last year founded a Ubrary known as the Transvaal Darjee Mandal Library, which is, and
wa be of great educational value and interest to the whole community. . The library is housed in the
all.
The Society has granted a scholarship of three hundred and eighty-seven rupees to Mr’ D.M. Dalal,
who-is doing his M.A. Class at St. Xavier’s College, Bombay.CYCLE HOUSE
CORNER MARSHALL AND VON BRANDIS. STREETS
JOHANNESBURG
Please Address’ Correspondence to P.O. Box 5765
Phones 22-4971 (4 lines) Tel. & Cable Add. ‘‘ELKAH”’A’sum of approximately £115 was collécted from the members and handed over to the Lourerico
Marques Bharat Samaj.
The Society is also’ trying to discourage those old traditions and empty shibboleths in social matters
which retard the progress of the community.
The Society has completely justified its existence, playing as it does an important and valuable
part in the cultural life ofthe Community. ‘Through its charitable and honest works it enjoys the confidence
and respect of those whom it serves.

11a, S.A. INDIAN COMMERCIAL DIRECTORY
THE VED MANDIR: erected for educational and social purposes by the Hindus at
Lourenco Marques. It is open to all Indian children irrespective of creed. The building
cost £5,000. and is the first of its kind to be erected in Portuguese East Africa. It was
‘formally opened by His Excellency the Governor-General of P.E.A. in 1938.
